title: Discrete-Time Signals and Systems
summary: This page introduces discrete-time signals as uniformly spaced samples of continuous-time signals, expressed using the notation x[n] = x(nT). It discusses natural occurrences of discrete-time signals in population studies, amortization, and radar tracking, as well as their use in digital filtering where continuous-time signals are processed by discrete-time systems via C/D and D/C conversion.

figures.caption: Processing continuous-time signals by discrete-time systems, where a continuous-time input x(t) is converted to a discrete-time signal x[n] via a C/D converter, processed by a discrete-time system to yield y[n], and then converted back to a continuous-time output y(t) via a D/C converter.
figures.description: The figure is a signal-processing block diagram with left-to-right arrows showing a continuous-time input x(t) entering a continuous-to-discrete converter labeled C/D, producing the discrete-time sequence x[n], then passing through a block labeled discrete-time system to produce y[n], and finally through a discrete-to-continuous converter labeled D/C to yield the continuous-time output y(t). Small sketches above the blocks illustrate the signal forms: smooth continuous curves for x(t) and y(t), and stem plots for x[n] and y[n], with dashed curves suggesting the underlying continuous waveform sampled at discrete instants. Students should notice the change in representation from continuous time to indexed samples and back, while the processing itself occurs in the discrete-time domain.
